title: How To Move A Prospect To A Client
summary: Erin Stetzer walked through her four-step framework for converting prospects into clients: creating common ground, showing genuine excitement, doing the work, and adding value through tangible deliverables. The session then opened to participant Q&A covering topics such as follow-up strategy after lost bids, handling price anxiety during presentations, positioning design-build advantages against lower-allowance competitors, and navigating pre-construction agreements.

teachings.explanation: Before investing time in an in-person meeting, every prospect should be filtered against your business model. If the project is a 'heck yes' fit, the meeting makes sense. If not, the conversation shifts to redirecting them to a better fit elsewhere. This protects your time and ensures you only pursue work you can truly serve.
teachings.quote: if it's filtered into 100 percent i'm super interested in this project then the meeting makes a lot of sense

teachings.explanation: In prospect meetings, the primary goal is to listen deeply rather than talk. You are waiting to identify the prospect's core needs—typically three to five—before responding. Speaking too soon, out of nerves or enthusiasm, undermines your ability to connect meaningfully and address what truly matters to the client.
teachings.quote: what you're really listening for the entire time is the need you're waiting and waiting and waiting to listen for the need or needs

teachings.explanation: Once you have identified a prospect's needs, respond with empathy before solutions. Clients are in a vulnerable position—they are investing heavily and fear being taken advantage of in an unregulated industry. Acknowledging their fear and sharing a relevant story builds the trust needed for a second conversation and ultimately a contract.
teachings.quote: you have to remember they're vulnerable they've they're investing in whoever they work with they really are in an unknown space

teachings.explanation: If a project has passed your filter, let your authentic enthusiasm show rather than guarding it out of fear of fee negotiation. Genuine excitement signals confidence and alignment, and it communicates that the project is a real fit for your business model—which itself becomes a selling point.
teachings.quote: i'm super excited for you guys and also this is this is a great fit for our business model and i want to talk about why

teachings.concept: Doing the Work to Build Competence and Confidence
teachings.explanation: Confidence in client meetings comes from deeply studying your work—analyzing budgets, reviewing plans, collaborating with subcontractors, and asking detailed questions. This investment creates observable competence that clients can see and feel, and it also produces institutional knowledge that improves your pricing accuracy and presentations over time.
teachings.quote: you just you got to do the work because in the work we understand the details and we ask the questions

teachings.explanation: Showing up to meetings with physical binders containing subcontractor bids, budgets, four-week schedules, weekly report templates, and process documents communicates professionalism without you having to say a word. These materials also serve as a baseline that educates clients on what a high-quality build process looks like, making them better equipped to evaluate other bids.
teachings.quote: people are tactical they're visual they want to know that you've done the work without you talking about it like they can see it

teachings.concept: Closing the Loop on Every Prospect
teachings.explanation: Whether you win or lose a project, always follow up to close the loop. After a meeting, send a text thank-you within an hour. If you lose a bid, request a brief feedback call. This discipline yields actionable intelligence for improving your systems, and it maintains relationships that may lead to future referrals.
teachings.quote: whenever we don't get a project we always follow up with hey do you have like 10 minutes just to like we really wanted to work with you could you give us 10 minutes and they do 100% of the time

teachings.concept: Price Certainty as a Design-Build Differentiator
teachings.explanation: Design-build firms that price from fully developed plans and specifications—rather than allowances—can offer clients near-certain budgets upfront. Framing this as peace of mind and a reduction in change-order risk, rather than defending a higher number, reframes the conversation positively and helps clients understand why lower allowance-based bids are misleading.
teachings.quote: I'm basically giving you price certainty right off the gate and I talk about how everybody else does allowances and I don't and I'm talking about that as a positive

teachings.explanation: By studying enough budgets over time, builders can identify reliable cost patterns—for example, that HVAC, electrical, and plumbing labor should fall within a predictable range of each other. Being able to explain and defend these patterns in a meeting demonstrates deep expertise and builds client confidence in your numbers.
teachings.quote: hey the HVAC electrical and plumbing labor they should all be within I don't know 10% of each other in terms of labor costs so look at your business and understand the patterns of pricing

actionable_advice: After every in-person prospect meeting, send a text thank-you within one hour to maintain momentum and demonstrate responsiveness.
Before speaking in a meeting, wait and collect at least three to five specific needs from the prospect before engaging with your response.
Bring a physical binder with subcontractor bids, budget documents, four-week schedules, and weekly report templates to every prospect presentation so clients can see your work visually.
When presenting a budget, ask the client upfront whether they want the bottom line first; if they show anxiety, go directly to the total before walking through details.
Keep budget-specific documents on a separate printed sheet so they can be updated to the last minute and referenced quickly during presentations.

Leave certain topics—such as modifications, budget-versus-actual tracking, and timelines—out of your initial presentation deliberately so clients have natural entry points to engage in conversation with you.
After losing a project, always request a 10-minute feedback call to understand why and use that information to improve your systems.
Continuously build competence by creating internal learning resources such as shared video libraries where project managers document field observations for the team.

When a design-build firm has designed the project themselves, position the pricing advantage explicitly: explain that you know the house inside and out and have been in dialogue with the architect and designer throughout, while competing builders have not.
Expand your referral network constantly by connecting clients, architects, designers, and builders to each other—even when the connection has nothing to do with a current project.
Be transparent with prospects about your project loading and availability so they can make timely decisions, framing it as honest communication rather than a sales tactic.
client_challenges: A participant asked how aggressively to pursue prospects who go silent after a meeting—whether to chase them or move on.
Tammy and Brett had a budget presentation the following day and were unsure how to handle prospects who are anxious to skip straight to the bottom-line number.
Matt asked whether the detailed deliverable work—subcontractor bids, binders, process documents—should only happen after a signed pre-construction agreement with a fee.
Sandy asked what common threads Erin has noticed in projects she did not win, and whether those losses led to process changes.

Tammy raised the challenge of being a design-build firm competing against builders using allowance-based floor-plan bids, where the design-build bid naturally comes in higher because it is based on fully developed plans and known selections.
Denise described a prospect who was clearly interested but absolutely refused to share her budget, making it impossible to scope the design appropriately.
A participant asked how to handle a client who wants to negotiate fees and maintain control over variable costs, and how to structure an alternative fee arrangement in response.

Martha raised the question of how FF&E procurement tracking works across the full lifecycle—from design and selection through ordering, receiving, inspecting, and delivery to the job site—and whether there is a better system than what her team had built.
notable_quotes: On active listening: 'what you're really listening for the entire time is the need you're waiting and waiting and waiting to listen for the need or needs'

On client vulnerability: 'they've they're investing in whoever they work with they really are in an unknown space and in our industry we can say that there are a lot it's not regulated and there are people that aren't honest right like that's just like with any business so they're scared of that they're wondering like can i even trust this person'
On showing up with physical deliverables: 'this seems so like 1980 but this is what people feel when you show up with a binder full of quotes that you've analyzed a proposal for them'
On the 10% who do the work: 'that's what people really want and 10% of the population will do it and be fine and excel and grow their companies and the other 90% won't'
On the design-build advantage: 'we have an unfair advantage we drafted the house and frankly we're just ahead of everyone so that's the gap'

On transparency over sales tactics: 'i'm not trying to rush you i'm trying to manage how we can serve our clients so if you think we can be helpful then we should connect pretty soon and that's the truth like that is the truth it's not trying to make a sale'
